specification.topic-statement: The JSON wrapper seam exposes JSON library operations as individually mockable link seams so that tests can intercept any JSON call without modifying production code.

scope.in-scope: Sixteen wrappers covering: reading a JSON document from a file path, reading a JSON document from a memory buffer, writing a mutable JSON document to a file path, traversing to the document root, looking up an object field by key, extracting a signed integer value, extracting a string value, creating a mutable object node, creating a mutable array node, adding a string field to a mutable object, adding a signed-integer field to a mutable object, adding an unsigned-integer field to a mutable object, adding a boolean field to a mutable object, adding an arbitrary value field to a mutable object, appending an arbitrary value to a mutable array, and performing a deep copy of an immutable value into a mutable document.
scope.boundary: Each wrapper delegates unconditionally to the corresponding JSON library operation. The wrapper adds no logic, transformation, or error handling of its own. The library's return value is passed through unchanged.

Tests define strong-symbol replacements for any subset of these wrappers. The strong definition shadows the default weak symbol, giving the test full control over that operation — for example, returning NULL from the parse wrapper to simulate a malformed-JSON failure path.

The file-reading and file-writing wrappers accept an optional custom allocator parameter that the JSON library uses for its internal allocations. Supplying a talloc-backed allocator (described separately in the JSON Allocator specification) causes all document memory to be tracked under a talloc context. The wrappers themselves impose no policy on this parameter.

behavior.build-mode-selection:
The wrappers exist in two forms depending on the build configuration.
In a debug or test build (when the NDEBUG macro is not defined), each wrapper is compiled as a weak symbol. A weak symbol has a default implementation but yields to any strong-symbol definition of the same name in the final link. This is the mechanism that makes individual wrappers replaceable in tests.
In a release build (when NDEBUG is defined), each wrapper is a static inline function defined in the header. No separate compilation unit is involved; the call is inlined at every call site with zero runtime overhead and no exported symbol.
The debug-build compilation unit that contains all sixteen wrapper bodies is marked to be excluded from line-coverage measurement. This is intentional: the wrappers are unconditional one-line delegators and measuring their coverage adds noise without signal.
behavior.document-reading-from-file: Reads a JSON document from the file at the given path. Accepts flags that control parsing strictness, an optional custom allocator for document memory, and an output struct that receives a detailed error description on failure. Returns a parsed document handle on success, or NULL on failure with the error struct populated.
behavior.document-reading-from-buffer: Reads a JSON document from the given byte buffer of the given length. Accepts flags that control parsing strictness. Uses the library's default allocator; there is no allocator parameter on this overload. Returns a parsed document handle on success, or NULL on failure.
behavior.document-writing-to-file: Writes a mutable JSON document to the file at the given path. Accepts flags that control serialization style (e.g., pretty-printing), an optional custom allocator for scratch memory during serialization, and an output struct that receives a detailed error description on failure. Returns true on success, false on failure.
behavior.root-access: Returns the root value of a parsed document. The document handle is the sole input. The return value is a borrowed pointer into the document's memory; its lifetime is bounded by the document's lifetime.
behavior.object-field-lookup: Looks up a field in a JSON object value by its string key using a linear scan. Returns the associated value if the key is present, or NULL if not found or if the input is not an object.
behavior.signed-integer-extraction: Extracts the signed 64-bit integer contained in a JSON number value. Returns the integer. Behavior is undefined if the value is not a numeric type or if the numeric value falls outside the 64-bit signed range.
behavior.string-extraction: Extracts the null-terminated string contents of a JSON string value. Returns a pointer into the document's internal storage; the pointer is valid for the lifetime of the document. Returns NULL if the value is not a string type.
behavior.mutable-object-creation: Creates an empty mutable JSON object node within a mutable document. The node's memory is owned by the document. Returns the node handle.
behavior.mutable-array-creation: Creates an empty mutable JSON array node within a mutable document. The node's memory is owned by the document. Returns the node handle.
behavior.mutable-object-string-field: Adds a key-value pair to a mutable object where the value is a string. The key and value strings are copied into the document's memory. Returns true on success, false if the operation fails.
behavior.mutable-object-signed-integer-field: Adds a key-value pair to a mutable object where the value is a signed 64-bit integer. Returns true on success, false on failure.
behavior.mutable-object-unsigned-integer-field: Adds a key-value pair to a mutable object where the value is an unsigned 64-bit integer. Returns true on success, false on failure.
behavior.mutable-object-boolean-field: Adds a key-value pair to a mutable object where the value is a boolean. Returns true on success, false on failure.
behavior.mutable-object-arbitrary-field: Adds a key-value pair to a mutable object where the value is an already-constructed mutable node (object, array, or scalar). The value node must belong to the same mutable document. Returns true on success, false on failure.
behavior.mutable-array-element-append: Appends an already-constructed mutable node to a mutable array. The node must belong to the same mutable document as the array. Returns true on success, false on failure.
behavior.immutable-to-mutable-copy: Performs a deep copy of an immutable JSON value (from a parsed document) into a mutable document, recursively duplicating all child nodes. Returns the root of the copied subtree as a mutable node owned by the target document, or NULL on failure.
behavior.test-override-pattern:
A test that needs to control a specific JSON operation defines a function with the exact same signature as the wrapper, without any weak attribute. Because the linker resolves strong symbols before weak ones, the test's definition becomes the active implementation for that link unit. The test can then consult file-local flags to decide whether to forward the call normally or return NULL to simulate a failure (e.g., a parse error or allocation failure inside the library).
File-local (static) state is the standard pattern for these flags, since each test file compiles into its own binary and tests within a file run sequentially.
